content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 ,J Street• Suite 620 • Sacr:imcnto, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Sarah E. Tobias 
Attorney at Law 
Goyette & Associates, Inc. 
1330 L Street, Suite G 
Fresno, CA 93 721 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-114 
Dear Ms. Tobias: 
May 11, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Tulare City Council Member 
Greg Nunley regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that the Commission is not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 
1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. We are 
only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other 
general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTIONS 
1. Would it constitute a conflict of interest if Council Member Nunley donated food from 
his privately owned restaurant, in his capacity as a private citizen only, to feed law enforcement 
officers and volunteers during the 24-hour vigil at the Tulare County Peace Officer Memorial? 
2. Are there any precautions Council Member Nunley should take to ensure his donation is 
known to be from him as a private citizen and business owner, such as making sure any signage or 
other information only makes reference to him as a private citizen or only uses the name of his 
restaurant? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. No. The Act does not affect the conduct of public officials acting in their capacity as 
private individuals. Rather, the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, 
or influencing a governmental decision if the public official has a financial interest in the decision. 
2. While not required, it may be prudent for the Council Member to clearly reflect that he is 
acting in his individual capacity as a private citizen. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-17-114 
Page No. 2 
Council Member Nunley was elected to the Tulare City Council in the November 8, 2016 
election. Council Member Nunley is also a business owner, land owner, and property developer in 
the City of Tulare. The Council Member owns a restaurant, Compadres, in the city. 
The Tulare Police Department is seeking assistance from local business owners in the food 
industry to help feed law enforcement officers and volunteers during an event starting on May 13, 
2017 and ending the morning of May 14, 2017. This event is a 24-hour vigil at the Tulare County 
Peace Officer Memorial. Specifically, the Tulare Police Department is seeking food donations for 
breakfast the morning of May 14, 2017 for the law enforcement officers and volunteers at this 
event. Council Member Nunley would like to donate food from his restaurant for this breakfast, 
solely in his capacity as a private citizen and business owner. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one 
or more of the public official's interests. Section 87103 also sets forth the interests from which a 
conflict of interest may arise under the Act. They include: 
• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 
or more. (Section 8 7103 (b ). ) 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $4 70 or 
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months 
prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).) 
• Personal finances, meaning the financial effect of a governmental decision on the personal 
finances of a public official or his or her immediate family. 
Your question involves Council Member Nunley's prospective donation of food from his 
privately owned restaurant (presumably Compadres) to feed law enforcement officers and 


File No. A-17-114 
Page No. 3 
volunteers during the 24-hour vigil at the Tulare County Peace Officer Memorial. Compadres 
would be an investment interest and a source of income to the Council Member. 
However, mere possession of a financial interest does not create a conflict of interest. 
Section 87100 requires that the official not make, participate in making, or use his or her official 
position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. The Act 
does not apply to private decisions of public officials, such as whether to provide private goods or 
resources to the government. The Act only prohibits the Council Member from making, 
participating in making or attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental 
decision in which he has a financial interest, and only then if his interest (the restaurant) would be 
foreseeably and materially financially affected by the governmental decision. Thus, no conflict of 
interest exists under these facts. 
You also asked if there are any precautions Council Member Nunley should take to ensure 
his donation is known to be from him as a private citizen and business owner. Since a conflict of 
interest does not exist, the Act would not require any specific action. However, it may be prudent 
for the Council Member to clearly reflect that he is acting in his individual capacity as a private 
citizen. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
Assi 
eral Counsel 
Le 
on

known to be from him as a private citizen and business owner, such as making sure any signage or 
other information only makes reference to him as a private citizen or only uses the name of his 
restaurant?

1. No. The Act does not affect the conduct of public officials acting in their capacity as 
private individuals. Rather, the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, 
or influencing a governmental decision if the public official has a financial interest in the decision. 
2. While not required, it may be prudent for the Council Member to clearly reflect that he is 
acting in his individual capacity as a private citizen.
